spec.question: How many home runs did Derek Jeter hit in May 1998?
spec.notes: Phase 1 Q1. 4 HRs: 5/2 vs KCA, 5/6 vs TEX, 5/13 vs TEX, 5/15 vs MIN.

questions.response_text:
Derek Jeter hit **4 home runs** in May 1998. That was part of his strong 1998 season with the Yankees, who went on to win the World Series that year.

Want a breakdown of those homers by date or opponent?

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(hr) AS home_runs
FROM retro.plays p
JOIN retro.games g ON p.gid = g.gid
WHERE p.batter = 'jeted001'
  AND g.gametype = 'regular'
  AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM g.date) = 1998
  AND EXTRACT(MONTH FROM g.date) = 5

spec.question: How many home runs did Mark McGwire hit in 1998?
spec.notes: Phase 1 Q2. Record-breaking season; #62 off Trachsel 9/8, #70 off Pavano 9/27.

questions.response_text: **70 home runs.** McGwire's 1998 season was one of the most celebrated in baseball history — he famously broke Roger Maris's single-season record of 61, finishing with 70 to edge out Sammy Sosa (who hit 66 that year) in one of the great home run races.

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(hr) AS home_runs
FROM retro.plays p
JOIN retro.games g ON p.gid = g.gid
WHERE p.batter = 'mcgwm001'
  AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM g.date) = 1998
  AND g.gametype = 'regular'

spec.question: Who were the best left-handed batters against left-handed pitchers in 1998?
spec.notes: Phase 1 Q3. Top with 150-AB floor: Olerud .375, Vaughn .340, Greer .328, Floyd .325, Vina .324. Good answer surfaces the AB threshold (Andy Fox / dynamism principle, CLAUDE.md §8). Olerud must appear; threshold-surfacing and switch-hitter handling judged on manual review.

questions.response_text:
Here's a fun leaderboard — these are lefty batters who absolutely *punished* left-handed pitching in 1998 (min. 50 PA vs. LHP):

---

### Best LHB vs. LHP, 1998 (min. 50 PA)

| Player | PA | AVG | OBP | SLG | HR |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **John Olerud** | 182 | **.375** | **.451** | .513 | 5 |
| Rusty Greer | 202 | .337 | .386 | .451 | 4 |
| Mo Vaughn | 220 | .333 | .377 | **.586** | 14 |
| Andy Fox | 109 | .333 | .376 | .427 | 0 |
| Will Clark | 166 | .327 | .373 | .517 | 5 |
| Cliff Floyd | 161 | .325 | .342 | .494 | 3 |
| Fernando Viña | 210 | .324 | .343 | .405 | 0 |
| Tony Gwynn | 173 | .323 | .358 | .534 | 8 |
| Larry Walker | 153 | .321 | **.405** | .511 | 3 |
| Rafael Palmeiro | 256 | .317 | .375 | .591 | 15 |
| **Ken Griffey Jr.** | 197 | .299 | .350 | **.701** | 21 |

---

**A few highlights:**

- **John Olerud** was a phenomenon here — .375/.451/.513 is an absurd line. He was famous for being a better hitter against same-handed pitching, which is the reverse of the normal platoon split.
- **Ken Griffey Jr.** wasn't at the top in average, but his .701 slugging with 21 HRs in 197 PA vs. LHP was devastating. That's nearly one HR every 9.4 PA.
- **Rafael Palmeiro** had the most PA (256) and was remarkably consistent — 15 HR with a .591 SLG.
- **Tony Gwynn** slugged .534 against lefties with 8 HR — way above his typical profile, showing lefties couldn't just throw him anything.

**For OPS**, the top three shake out as: Griffey (1.051), Olerud (0.964), Palmeiro (0.966) — all elite, very different profiles.

Want me to dig deeper on anyone here, or look at a lower PA threshold to catch part-timers who really raked?

tool_input.query:
SELECT 
    p.batter,
    pl.usename || ' ' || pl.lastname AS player_name,
    SUM(p.pa) AS pa,
    SUM(p.ab) AS ab,
    SUM(p.single + p.double + p.triple + p.hr) AS hits,
    SUM(p.hr) AS hr,
    SUM(p.walk) AS bb,
    SUM(p.k) AS k,
    SUM(p.single) AS singles,
    SUM(p.double) AS doubles,
    SUM(p.triple) AS triples,
    ROUND(SUM(p.single + p.double + p.triple + p.hr)::numeric / NULLIF(SUM(p.ab), 0), 3) AS avg,
    ROUND((SUM(p.single + p.double + p.triple + p.hr) + SUM(p.walk))::numeric / NULLIF(SUM(p.pa), 0), 3) AS obp,
    ROUND((SUM(p.single) + 2*SUM(p.double) + 3*SUM(p.triple) + 4*SUM(p.hr))::numeric / NULLIF(SUM(p.ab), 0), 3) AS slg
FROM retro.plays p
JOIN retro.games g ON p.gid = g.gid
JOIN retro.players pl ON p.batter = pl.id
WHERE g.gametype = 'regular'
    AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM g.date) = 1998
    AND p.bathand = 'L'
    AND p.pithand = 'L'
GROUP BY p.batter, pl.usename, pl.lastname
HAVING SUM(p.pa) >= 50
ORDER BY avg DESC
LIMIT 20;

spec.question: Who hit for the cycle in 1998?
spec.notes: Phase 1 Q4. Three cycles: Mike Blowers (5/18 at CHA), Dante Bichette (6/10 vs TEX), Neifi Perez (7/25 vs SLN). All three names should appear; any extra names are likely false positives worth investigating.

questions.response_text:
Three players hit for the cycle in 1998:

| Date | Player | Team | Opponent |
|------|--------|------|----------|
| May 18 | **Mike Blowers** | Oakland A's | Chicago White Sox |
| June 10 | **Dante Bichette** | Colorado Rockies | Texas Rangers |
| July 25 | **Neifi Perez** | Colorado Rockies | St. Louis Cardinals |

A couple of fun notes: two of the three happened at Coors Field in Colorado, and Neifi Perez was just 24 years old when he did it. Blowers' cycle came against the White Sox in Chicago.

Want me to dig into any of the individual games — like the exact hit sequence or inning breakdowns?

tool_input.query:
SELECT p.batter, pl.usename, pl.lastname, p.gid, g.date, p.batteam, p.pitteam
FROM retro.plays p
JOIN retro.games g ON p.gid = g.gid
JOIN retro.players pl ON p.batter = pl.id
WHERE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM g.date) = 1998
  AND g.gametype = 'regular'
GROUP BY p.batter, pl.usename, pl.lastname, p.gid, g.date, p.batteam, p.pitteam
HAVING SUM(p.single) >= 1
   AND SUM(p.double) >= 1
   AND SUM(p.triple) >= 1
   AND SUM(p.hr) >= 1
ORDER BY g.date;

spec.question: What was the 1998 Yankees' regular-season record?
spec.notes: Phase 1 Q5. 162 games, 114-48, .704. Substrings allow varied phrasings ('114 wins, 48 losses', '114-48').

questions.response_text: The 1998 Yankees went **114-48** in the regular season — one of the best records in AL history. That .704 winning percentage powered them to the World Series title that year, where they swept the Padres. A genuinely legendary team.

tool_input.query:
SELECT
  SUM(CASE WHEN wteam = 'NYA' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS wins,
  SUM(CASE WHEN lteam = 'NYA' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS losses
FROM retro.games
WHERE (hometeam = 'NYA' OR visteam = 'NYA')
  AND gametype = 'regular'
  AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM date) = 1998

spec.question: How many career home runs did Babe Ruth hit?

questions.response_text:
There it is — the canonical **714 regular-season home runs**, confirmed from the data. Here's the full breakdown:

| Game Type | HRs |
|---|---|
| Regular Season | **714** |
| World Series | 15 |
| All-Star | 1 |
| **Total** | **730** |

The 714 regular-season figure stood as the all-time MLB record for nearly four decades until Hank Aaron broke it in 1974. Ruth also clubbed 15 World Series homers, which is itself a record that still stands today.

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(hr) AS career_hr
FROM retro.plays
WHERE batter = 'ruthb101'

SELECT g.gametype, SUM(p.hr) AS hr_total
FROM retro.plays p
JOIN retro.games g ON p.gid = g.gid
WHERE p.batter = 'ruthb101'
GROUP BY g.gametype
ORDER BY g.gametype

spec.question: How many career home runs did Hank Aaron hit?

questions.response_text: **755 home runs** — the number Hank Aaron carried into the record books. He held the all-time MLB career home run record from April 8, 1974 (when he passed Babe Ruth's 714) until Barry Bonds surpassed him in 2007. Aaron hit those 755 over a 23-year career from 1954 to 1976, almost entirely with the Braves (Milwaukee and Atlanta) plus a final two seasons back in Milwaukee with the Brewers.

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(b_hr) AS career_hr
FROM retro.batting
WHERE id = 'aaroh101'
  AND stattype = 'value'
  AND gid IN (SELECT gid FROM retro.games WHERE gametype = 'regular')

spec.question: How many home runs did Barry Bonds hit in 2001?

questions.response_text: **73.** Barry Bonds hit 73 home runs in the 2001 regular season, setting the all-time single-season record. It's still the mark to beat today.

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(hr) AS home_runs
FROM retro.plays
JOIN retro.games ON plays.gid = games.gid
WHERE plays.batter = 'bondb001'
  AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM games.date) = 2001
  AND games.gametype = 'regular'

spec.question: How many hits did Ichiro Suzuki get in 2004?
spec.notes: Phase 2 Layer 2 verified: single-season hits record, 262.

questions.response_text: Ichiro had **262 hits** in 2004 — the all-time single-season record, breaking George Sisler's mark of 257 set in 1920. It remains one of the most celebrated offensive milestones in baseball history.

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(b_h) AS hits
FROM retro.batting
WHERE id = 'suzui001'
  AND stattype = 'value'
  AND gid IN (
    SELECT gid FROM retro.games
    WHERE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM date) = 2004
      AND gametype = 'regular'
  )

spec.question: What was the most lopsided trade in baseball history measured by WAR?
spec.notes: Retrosheet does not include WAR or trade records (CLAUDE.md §7). Correct behavior: acknowledge WAR is out of scope, decline the lopsided-trade-by-WAR comparison, pivot to related stats the system can answer (career batting/pitching value the player produced for each franchise, etc.). Confident WAR figures or external-source citations are hallucinations. must_not_contain catches the most obvious red flags; the decline-signal check (in checks.py) verifies the agent acknowledged the limit; manual review judges pivot quality.

questions.response_text:
WAR isn't part of the Retrosheet dataset — it's a calculated metric (Wins Above Replacement) that isn't stored in the play-by-play corpus I query. So I can't directly rank trades by WAR differential.

What I *can* do is pull career or post-trade counting and rate stats for both sides of any specific trade you're curious about. That won't give you WAR, but games played, OPS, ERA, wins, etc. can tell a pretty clear story about who got the better end of a deal.

A few trades that are historically famous for being one-sided that I could dig into:

- **Nolan Ryan for Jim Fregosi** (Mets → Angels, 1971)
- **Frank Robinson for Mitch Pappas et al.** (Reds → Orioles, 1965)
- **Lou Brock for Ernie Broglio** (Cubs → Cardinals, 1964)
- **Jeff Bagwell for Larry Andersen** (Red Sox → Astros, 1990)
- **Roberto Clemente passing through waivers** to the Pirates from the Dodgers (1954)

Want me to pull post-trade stats for one (or all) of these, or is there a specific trade you had in mind?
